sound_type: churring call -- harsh, dry, rolling raspy series. Barely drums. The quietest acoustic profile of any Picidae in the streak. E=4.
ecosystem: Open ponderosa pine forest, burned forest with standing snags, oak-pine woodland. Fire-dependent habitat. Post-fire specialist.
season: Summer visitor to PNW (eastern Cascades). Some year-round in southern range. Migratory or partially migratory.

coordinates.description: Reference for eastern Cascades open pine habitat. Lewis's Woodpeckers found in open ponderosa pine forest and burned areas on the east side of the Cascades.

taxonomy_note.description: Sixth consecutive Picidae (107-112). One short of the Corvidae septet record (100-106). Melanerpes lewis -- same genus as Acorn Woodpecker (107) but radically different strategy. The flycatching woodpecker: catches insects on the wing, rarely drums, rarely drills. Named for Meriwether Lewis of the Lewis and Clark Expedition (1805). Declining species -- 50-70% population loss due to fire suppression. E=4.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Picidae count: 9 entries (Northern Flicker 21-22, Red-breasted Sapsucker 54, Pileated Woodpecker 54, Acorn Woodpecker 107, Pileated Woodpecker laughing 108, Pileated Woodpecker drumming 109, Hairy Woodpecker 110, Downy Woodpecker 111, Lewis's Woodpecker 112)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Melanerpes from Greek melanos ('black') + herpes ('creeper') -- 'black creeper.' lewis honors Captain Meriwether Lewis, who collected the type specimen November 20, 1805, along the Clearwater River in Idaho. Lewis's Woodpecker: the black creeper named for Lewis.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Melanerpes -- same genus as the Acorn Woodpecker (degree 107). Two Melanerpes species in the engine with radically different ecological strategies: Acorn (colonial, granary, social) vs. Lewis's (solitary, crevice-caching, flycatching).
taxonomy_note.order_note: Piciformes. Sixth consecutive (107-112). One short of the Corvidae septet record. The longest Picidae streak in the engine.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Picidae continues for a sixth consecutive degree. One short of the all-time family-streak record (Corvidae septet, 100-106, seven consecutive).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 1,000-4,000 Hz. Broadly distributed. The churring quality comes from rapid frequency modulation.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 1,500-3,000 Hz. The harsh, dry churring has energy spread across a broad band.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Churring call: 1,000-4,000 Hz, harsh, dry, rolling. Rarely drums; drum when present is weak and irregular.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Churring call: 1-3 seconds. A sustained rolling sound. Drum (rare): weak, irregular, brief.
song_metrics.note_structure: CHURRING: Harsh, rolling, raspy series of notes. Dry and grating quality. Not musical. Compared to a squeaky gate or dry rattle.
song_metrics.note_types: Churring call (primary, contact/territorial), occasional weak drum (rare, vestigial). The churring call is the diagnostic vocalization.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Sustained churring series. Simple structure. No repeated phrases or complex patterns.
song_metrics.timbre: Harsh. Dry. Churring. Grating. The most unmusical vocalization in the Picidae streak. Not sharp (like Hairy), not soft (like Downy), not ringing (like Pileated). Rough and dry.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4 -- MODERATE energy. The churring call is not particularly loud. The near-absence of drumming further reduces acoustic footprint. A quiet woodpecker.
song_metrics.tempo: The churring series is rolling and continuous, not sharply rhythmic. Moderate tempo.
song_metrics.context: CONTACT AND TERRITORIAL. Churring call used for contact between mates and territorial signaling. Less acoustically territorial than other woodpeckers -- relies less on drumming for territory establishment.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. Churring call is consistent across range.
song_metrics.key_property: CHURRING CALL -- harsh, dry, rolling. E=4. BARELY DRUMS -- the woodpecker that has largely abandoned drumming. FLYCATCHING -- catches insects on the wing, not by drilling. Sixth consecutive Picidae (107-112). One short of record. Named for Meriwether Lewis (1805). DECLINING SPECIES -- 50-70% population loss. Fire suppression removing post-fire habitat.

plumage.overall: Medium-large woodpecker. Dark greenish-black back with iridescent quality. Gray collar and breast. Pink-red belly (distinctive -- no other NA woodpecker has pink belly). Dark red face. No bold black-and-white pattern. Crow-like overall impression.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 112: (1) PINK-RED BELLY -- unique among North American woodpeckers. (2) DARK GREENISH-BLACK BACK -- iridescent, no white markings. (3) GRAY COLLAR/BREAST -- unusual gray tones. (4) CROW-LIKE FLIGHT -- broad wings, direct (not undulating). (5) EXPOSED PERCHING -- sits on perches like a flycatcher, not clinging to trunks.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal. Sexes similar (both sexes have red face and pink belly -- unlike most woodpeckers where only males show red).
plumage.flight_pattern: Broad-winged, slow, direct. Unlike the undulating flight of most woodpeckers. The direct, crow-like flight is diagnostic.

physical_description.overall: Medium-large woodpecker. Body length 26-28 cm. Wingspan 49-52 cm. Weight 88-138 g. Dark greenish-black above, gray collar/breast, pink-red belly, dark red face. Short curved bill. Broad wings.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 112: dark woodpecker with pink belly and direct (not undulating) flight in open pine or burned forest habitat. If a dark, broad-winged bird flies straight across open forest without undulating and has a pink belly -- it's a Lewis's Woodpecker.
physical_description.field_id: Dark, crow-like woodpecker with pink belly and direct flight. Sits on exposed perches in open pine habitat. Catches insects on the wing. Churring call. Does not undulate in flight.

nodes.title: The Flycatching Woodpecker -- Evolved Past Drilling
nodes.description: Catches insects on the wing by sallying from exposed perches. Rarely drums. Rarely drills. A woodpecker that has largely abandoned the woodpecker lifestyle. Open-air foraging in open pine habitat. The most behaviorally anomalous woodpecker in North America.

nodes.title: Declining Species -- 50-70% Population Loss
nodes.description: Fire suppression has removed post-fire habitat. Open ponderosa pine and burned forest disappearing. Partners in Flight Watch List. Conservation depends on restoring fire to western forests. Contrast with Downy (111) which thrives everywhere.

nodes.title: Fire-Dependent Habitat -- Post-Fire Specialist
nodes.description: Requires open ponderosa pine, burned forest with snags, oak-pine woodland. Fire creates the habitat. Fire suppression destroys it. Post-fire landscapes with abundant snags are prime Lewis's Woodpecker territory.

nodes.title: Named for Meriwether Lewis -- Corps of Discovery, 1805
nodes.description: Captain Meriwether Lewis collected the type specimen November 20, 1805, Clearwater River, Idaho. Lewis and Clark Expedition (1804-1806). Clark's Nutcracker at degree 104. Both expedition-named species in the engine, eight degrees apart.

nodes.title: Sixth Consecutive Picidae (107-112)
nodes.description: Acorn (107), Pileated laughing (108), Pileated drumming (109), Hairy (110), Downy (111), Lewis's (112). Six consecutive. One short of the Corvidae septet record (100-106, seven). The streak explores Picidae to its behavioral edges.

nodes.title: Melanerpes Genus -- Acorn and Lewis's Compared
nodes.description: Both Melanerpes. Acorn Woodpecker (107): colonial, granary-building, social, E=5. Lewis's Woodpecker (112): solitary, crevice-caching, flycatching, E=4. Same genus, radically different ecological strategies.

cross_references.relationship: CONSERVATION CONTRAST. Both E=4. Downy thrives everywhere including suburbs -- population stable/increasing. Lewis's declining 50-70% -- fire-dependent habitat disappearing. Same energy, opposite trajectories.
SAME GENUS (Melanerpes). Acorn: colonial, granary-building, social (E=5). Lewis's: solitary, crevice-caching, flycatching (E=4). Same genus, radically different strategies.
PICIDAE BEHAVIORAL RANGE. Pileated: quintessential driller, thunderous drumming, massive cavities (E=6). Lewis's: barely drills, barely drums, flycatches (E=4). Opposite ends of Picidae behavior.
LEWIS AND CLARK CONNECTION. Lewis's Woodpecker (Meriwether Lewis, 1805) and Clark's Nutcracker (William Clark). Both Corps of Discovery namesakes. Eight degrees apart (104, 112).
NON-STANDARD FORAGING. Flicker forages on ground for ants. Lewis's flycatches on wing. Two Picidae that have evolved away from typical woodpecker drilling.
